data.title: Heathrow boss: Airport isn’t crowded... foreigners just walk the wrong way
data.description:
The chief executive of Heathrow has said the airport is not crowded because foreigners just walk the wrong way.

Thomas Woldbye said people complain the airport is too busy when in fact people are just “crashing into each other”.

This is because European travellers are used to walking on the right, while the British walk on the left.

Mr Woldbye told an Aviation Club UK event on Thursday: “One of the reasons people think [Terminal 5] is crowded – it’s not, actually. But yes, people are in the wrong place.

“Problem is that all the British people keep to the left and normally Europeans keep to the right. And they do that in both directions. So we can be crashing into each other and I see that from personal experience.

“We just [need to] make sure that everybody going this way keeps going left, and [those] going this way keep to the right,” he added, gesturing with his hands to illustrate the different directions.

The humorous aside came after the Danish-born airport boss warned earlier this week that Heathrow could lose its coveted spot as Europe’s busiest airport to rivals such as Amsterdam or Paris unless a third runway is approved for construction.

Mr Woldbye said on Tuesday: “We remain Europe’s largest airport but latest figures show we may lose that position in 2026 and we cannot keep driving growth for the UK economy without more capacity.”

When asked what he thought of the Government’s position on the third runway, he said it was broadly supportive. But he added: “We don’t have long-term regulation, which is clear in terms of us building a business case.”

Last year, Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, gave the go-ahead to Heathrow’s preferred plan to build a third runway over the top of the M25 motorway, a scheme likely to generate controversy over its cost and complexity.

The third runway scheme, which will see a 3,500-metre strip built to the north-west of Heathrow’s existing site, is currently estimated to cost around £49bn.

Included in the controversial plans are a scheme to build a 66,000-space car park at a cost of £2.6bn, as revealed by the City AM financial newspaper last summer.

The airport boss was also criticised on Thursday for raising drop-off fees paid by drivers to £7 – and threatening an £80 fine for motorists spending longer than 10 minutes saying goodbye to their loved ones.

He said in response to the criticism: “So when we make profits on all parts of the business, rather than just running the airport, that actually goes to reducing the charge [applied to passengers’ tickets].

“Whenever we make less money ... passengers are actually paying [for] that,” he added.

“We have an incentive to make as much money as we can, on the side, from anything that actually reduces the charge for passengers.”

Heathrow’s charges are regulated by the Government. The airport pays for its infrastructure by imposing a charge on airline passengers’ tickets.

Airlines, including Virgin Atlantic and British Airways, have set up a pressure group called Heathrow Reimagined, which calls for “urgent and fundamental reform” of this funding system.

data.title: Another shadow bank hit by sell-off as AI fears spread
data.description:
The world’s biggest “shadow bank” fund has been hit by a record sell-off amid fears about the impact of AI on companies backed by private loans.

Blackstone’s private credit fund, known as Bcred, saw investors pull $3.8bn (£2.9bn) in the last quarter – equivalent to 7.9pc of its total shares.

The fund typically limits withdrawals to 5pc per quarter, meaning that Blackstone – a “shadow bank” that acts like a lender but is unregulated – has been forced to change its rules to meet the requests. Bcred is the world’s largest private credit fund with $82bn of assets under management.

The move highlights investors’ heightened fears about the impact of AI on the tech companies the fund has lent to.

Software companies have been heavily backed by private credit funds in recent years, but the rise of Anthropic’s Claude and ChatGPT has raised questions about their future.

That poses questions for private credit lenders, who must ensure the companies they lend to are viable enough to repay the loans.

Shareholders in a fund focused on technology managed by Blue Owl, another major player in private credit, redeemed shares worth about 15pc of its net assets in its most recent quarter.

Another fund managed by Blue Owl opted to halt quarterly redemptions and started selling assets to return capital to investors.

Blackstone said it would meet requests by increasing its redemption limit to 7pc of the fund’s total shares. To make up the rest, the private equity firm, alongside employees, will offset the remaining 0.9pc.

Lloyd Blankfein, who led Goldman Sachs through the 2008 financial crash, said this week that he saw parallels between the boom in private credit and the global financial crisis.

“I wonder where there’s hidden secret leverage,” he said.

“Now everyone says, ‘Oh, the world’s not leveraged.’ That’s exactly what everybody said in the mortgage crisis until you suddenly discover that there was a lot of mortgage risk in Iceland.”

“It sort of smells like that kind of moment again,” Mr Blankfein added. “I don’t feel the storm, but the horses are starting to whinny in the corral.”

His comments echoed similar warnings from Jamie Dimon, the chief executive of JPMorgan, who was among the first to note the growing number of so-called “cockroaches” in private credit.

“Unfortunately, we did see this in ’05, ’06 and ’07 – almost the same thing – the rising tide was lifting all boats. Everyone was making a lot of money,” Mr Dimon said last week. “I see a couple of people doing some dumb things.”

Britain’s private market has grown by 56pc since 2015 to $185bn and is the second largest after the US, according to a recent report by the House of Lords.

The sector’s rapid growth has raised concerns about its stability and the potential impact of a crash on the wider financial system.

In the UK, the Bank of England plans to conduct the world’s first “stress test” of the shadow banking sector to see how it would cope with a global shock next year.

data.title: Nissan warns of risk to UK factory from Brussels boycott
data.description:
Ministers are holding urgent talks with carmakers after the owner of Britain’s biggest vehicle plant Nissan warned an EU boycott threatened its future.

The car giant, which employs 6,000 workers in Sunderland, has reportedly warned the Government that new “made in Europe” rules proposed by Brussels pose an “existential” risk to its operations.

The EU scheme is expected to treat most UK-made goods imported into the trading bloc as EU-equivalent – but British carmakers will largely be excluded amid a French-led effort to lock them out of the system.

Such a move would threaten to make them uncompetitive on the Continent, which is a major export market for UK automotive manufacturers.

A senior car industry insider warned that corporate fleet purchases – which alone account for three fifths of EU new car sales – would only qualify for subsidies if the vehicles are assembled in the bloc.

That is a potentially major problem for Nissan’s plant in Sunderland, where the vast majority of vehicles produced for export across the Channel.

Nissan executives have warned ministers that the rules are of serious concern, industry and Whitehall sources told The Telegraph.

The Financial Times said they had also threatened to close the Sunderland plant if the “existential threat” was not addressed – a claim strongly denied by sources close to the company on Thursday night.

‘Unnecessary complexity’

However, in a statement Nissan criticised the EU proposals and claimed they would “create confusion and add unnecessary complexity”.

Representatives from the Japanese-owned company met with Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, during his visit to Brussels last week, along with other carmakers.

Mr Kyle has been intensely lobbying EU officials to adopt a more friendly approach to British carmakers. A Whitehall source said ministers remained in close contact with the industry.

The source said talks with EU officials had been “good and constructive” so far, adding: “We do not see this as the end of the road. We are pushing to get the best outcome for Britain’s automotive sector.”

But the Society for Motor Manufacturers and Traders (SMMT) warned that carmakers were “gravely concerned” by the plans and called on the Government to “work urgently” with Brussels to solve the problem.

The UK automotive industry exports 80pc of its output, and the EU buys more than half of that. The industry estimates that up to 60pc of the £70bn annual car trade between Britain and the EU could be at stake.

British officials and executives initially welcomed the EU’s new rules, which were released on Wednesday in a draft “Industrial Accelerator Act”, as it looked like Mr Kyle’s lobbying efforts had successfully won the UK preferred status. 

However, carmakers said that the fine print soon revealed that a push by France and certain other member states meant British automotive manufacturers would be excluded from subsidies covering corporate fleets and small cars.

Britain also plays a crucial role in providing parts and components for European electric vehicles (EVs), and would be ruled out of the EU’s new project to create a “Small Affordable EV.”

The EU’s proposals are aimed at protecting the Continent’s manufacturers from foreign rivals – most notably China – by closing off European public contracts or subsidies to non-EU firms.

But the the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders (SMMT) has warned that UK carmakers risk being caught in the crossfire of the “protectionist” rules.

Mike Hawes, chief executive of the SMMT, said: “The criteria currently proposed would effectively put UK manufacturers at a systemic competitive disadvantage in the EU market.”

It could also be a breach of the UK-EU post-Brexit trade deal, he said.

The Government would not comment on the scheme’s impact on cars.

Ministers may seek to find a solution in time for Prime Minister Keir Starmer’s next summit with EU leaders, expected before the summer.

“Now is the time to work together as like-minded partners to boost growth, resilience, and economic security,” a Government spokesman said.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: ‘What does the RSC really need? A better train line to Stratford’
data.description:
I meet Daniel Evans and Tamara Harvey on the morning they announce their biggest coup to date – bringing Game of Thrones to the Stratford main stage this summer. The Mad King, a prequel play to the epic fantasy saga, has been written with the blessing and input of author George RR Martin. The news coincides, oddly, with the dramatic arrest of Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor (“Very Westeros,” Evans quips).

Have the two artistic directors of the Royal Shakespeare Company hit on the holy grail – the next Stranger Things: First Shadow, perhaps or Harry Potter and the Cursed Child? “That thinking is dangerous,” Evans, 52, counters. “The minute you think like that, you can jinx it.

“The reason we’re passionate about this is because it reads like a Shakespeare history play. It concerns leadership, succession, power, internecine fighting. George RR Martin is obsessed with Shakespeare.”

The globally successful HBO series (2011-19) was notorious for its scenes of sex and violence – it carried an 18 certificate in Britain. The prequel centres on the fabled Great Tourney [jousting tournament] at Harrenhal castle, and the tyrant king Aerys II Targaryen. “It’s going to be total theatre,” promises Evans of the production, which will be directed by Dominic Cooke (Hello, Dolly!). “There will be a content advisory, but we’re expecting it to be something the whole family can enjoy.”

The pair also tell me about the next wave of RSC programming for the autumn and winter which is announced today. This includes an all-male production of As You Like It (directed by Evans), Harriet Walter reprising her star turn as Brutus in a 90-minute version of Phyllida Lloyd’s acclaimed all-female Julius Caesar, plus a two-part adaptation of Middlemarch from Nina Raine. The year looks set to end on a swashbuckling high with The Three Musketeers, in which Dumas’s musketeers will be – gasp! – female.

Evans and Harvey, are a seamless and courteous double act. Having both enjoyed success running regional theatres (him: Sheffield and Chichester; her: Theatr Clwyd in Wales), they bonded during the stresses of the pandemic, and applied for one of the biggest jobs in British theatre on a joint ticket in 2022, succeeding Erica Whyman, who was briefly promoted from deputy after 10 years of Gregory Doran at the helm. Their tenure began properly in 2024. It’s full-on stuff, with Harvey, 48, talking about days that run from dawn to midnight, with her family commitments as a mother of two interspersed: “We often wish we could clone ourselves.”

In a departure from longstanding RSC man Doran’s methodical march through the canon, they have modified the approach so that it’s now more about bringing in diverse artistic talents with fresh, exciting ideas about what to stage, and how to stage it. As Harvey explains: “For us, there’s no point in putting on King John just because it hasn’t been done for a few years.”

The new rule is that there is no rule – anything could, conceivably, be an RSC show. While that approach might alarm traditionalists, it has reaped dividends in terms of ticket sales. More than 352,000 people attended productions in Stratford in their first year and The BFG last Christmas was the highest-grossing play in the history of the RSC. Rupert Goold’s Hamlet also played to 90 per cent capacity, while Kenneth Branagh’s imminent return to the RSC for the first time in more than 30 years in The Tempest has already sold out, just as Macbeth, starring Outlander’s Sam Heughan, did. Cyrano de Bergerac, starring Adrian Lester, is transferring to the West End this summer on the back of rave reviews.

A recent hit, Measure for Measure at Stratford, played fast and loose with the text – what do they think is acceptable in terms of tweaking texts many regard as sacrosanct? Evans tells me: “It’s OK to rewrite Shakespeare, but it’s not a free-for-all. Give us your rationale. The rule is that we’re not opposed to it. We’re not purists.” Harvey chips in with thoughts fresh from her rehearsals on Henry V, which she is about to bring to the Stratford main stage, with Alfred Enoch in the lead. “You can pick up any Arden edition and the footnotes read like a food fight between different editors,” she says. In her staging, too, she’s feminising some parts: “I didn’t want it to be a world where we assumed that almost everyone is male other than Katharine and Queen Isabel.”

One could easily spend an hour talking about Henry V (Harvey is, shockingly, the first woman to direct the play in the RSC’s history), but there are other serious matters to discuss.

Earlier this month, Trevor Nunn, the former RSC chief, stated that the RSC needed a London home; its purpose-built abode, the Barbican, is now one of a number of venues taking RSC work. “We are working out what London means for us,” says Harvey.

The more pressing task is transport to Stratford, which can seem Elizabethan in its remoteness. “To be frank,” Evans says, “a direct and better train service from London would be transformational. There used to be a direct train to Stratford [until 2023]. There no longer is. And we suffer because of it. When you look at theatre programmes from the 1960s, they used to have the last train time.” Could transport failure scupper their mission? “It might, because if we can’t get people to Stratford, how on earth are we meant to put on plays?”

That sounds quite existential, and behind the upbeat news there are clearly pressing challenges. Dividends reaped from ticket sales have not been substantial enough to mitigate the financial strain on the organisation, which is looking to reduce its workforce to achieve annual savings of £2.8m. In the autumn, the RSC announced that 420 of its 835 employees were eligible for voluntary redundancy – that consultation period is almost complete. The accounts for 2024-25 highlight the deficit problem: total income was £82m, substantially down on the previous year, with total expenditure running over £84m. Since then, annual repayments of the £20m Culture Recovery Fund loan taken out during Covid have kicked in, too. The drop in income partly reflects previous bumper takings at the box office – the 2022 Matilda film helped boost audiences for the stage show. But a cluster of factors are making life tougher.

“If you talk to most theatre leaders up and down the country, this is the case for them too,” Evans says. “Audiences have less money to spend because of the cost of living crisis. Costs have gone up exponentially. And there has long been a stand-still in Arts Council funding [currently approx £15m], which means a real-terms cut.”

There are no magic wands. Shows such as Les Misérables – which bankrolled the RSC for decades – are, he says, “like gold-dust – you’d be a fool to rely on them and part of the work we’re doing [in cutting back expenditure] is insulating the RSC from that reliance”. The desirability of any given corporate sponsor aside (and the RSC withdrew from a BP sponsorship deal in 2019 following protests), those options have dwindled, too: “There’s less money. Wherever the money comes from, there’s a deeper issue about relying on corporate sponsorship now.”

One option would be to scale back on productions, but that isn’t in their game plan, Harvey argues. “We have to find different ways of working but a sure-fire way to oblivion would be to reduce our artistic ambition and do less because then there’d be no point in our existing. You can cut back or you can be ever more ambitious and find different ways of making work and we’re going that route.”

There have been rumblings of discontent, particularly over plans to close the music department, with its in-house musicians. An online petition – attracting almost 7,000 signatures – has called for a rethink. If enough people complain, will they change their position? Harvey is swift to justify the proposed changes: “It’s about ensuring that music is more fully integrated into how we make work and giving composers the opportunity to work with freelance musicians. Some music will be created electronically, some will be recorded, some of it will be live.”

Upheavals notwithstanding, they’re looking to the future, with Evans (also an actor who left drama school early in order to join the RSC in the 1990s), hinting at repertory ensemble models. “We want to keep surprising audiences,” says Harvey.

As the actors bound back into the spacious Ashcroft room to resume rehearsals for Henry V, she leaves me with a final thought. Whenever the weight of responsibility presses hard, she confides, “I love to remember that this place exists because in the 1870s a local brewer and his son thought there should be a theatre in Stratford. The town and the theatre industry told them that they were mad but they understood that these plays survive because they’re performed. I love to remember that right at the root of this is a local brewer who had a radical idea, and the passion to see it through.”

Priority booking for all newly announced productions opens March 2 at 10am, with public booking opening March 18 at 10am. Priority booking for Game of Thrones: The Mad King opens April 14 with public booking from April 24. Info: rsc.org.uk

data.title: Daily Mirror-owner suffers biggest loss in a decade as Google traffic slumps
data.description:
The Daily Mirror’s owner has slumped to its biggest loss in more than a decade after a collapse in Google traffic led to a steep drop in digital readership.

Reach, formerly known as Trinity Mirror, fell £165m into the red after warning of a “sharp decline in referral traffic” from the search giant.

The loss, which reversed a £63m profit last year, was driven by a £223m impairment charge following a decline in visitors from Google’s search engine, which cut growth forecasts.

“The unhelpful referrer and macro environments have tempered our view on digital growth over the near term,” said Piers North, Reach’s chief executive.

The loss for the publisher of the Daily Mirror, Express and dozens of regional newspapers is the deepest of the Reach era.

The business was rebranded from Trinity Mirror in 2018 after the purchase of the Express titles from media mogul Richard Desmond. Trinity Mirror last reported a similar-sized loss in 2014, when it fell £160m into the red.

The company has been grappling with Google’s shifting algorithm, which has threatened traffic to free-to-read websites.

Google’s introduction of “AI Overviews”, which give short descriptions of news topics, has left media sites scrambling to attract visitors as more readers turn to the search engine for information or advice.

Data from Ipsos for January showed that Reach had suffered a collapse in page views for some of its biggest local titles, which have suffered from Google’s AI shift.

Page views of Surrey Live tumbled almost 85pc, Leeds Live’s page views fell by 77pc, and Bristol Live dropped just under 70pc.

Despite the falls, Reach hailed its own use of AI, claiming that a tool called “Guten”, which rehashes stories from across its titles, is used for 26pc of its articles.

The publisher has been forced to push into subscriptions for its red top and regional papers, adding premium subscriptions to titles including the Manchester Evening News, Liverpool Echo and WalesOnline.

Reach said it had so far seen about 15,000 subscribers to its premium subscription products and was targeting about 75,000 digital subscribers this year.

Last September, Reach announced 321 job cuts, including 186 editorial roles. At the time, it said it intended to create 135 new jobs, mainly in video production.

Unions have claimed that Reach’s decision to shut most of its printing presses has put more than 250 jobs at risk.

Sharon Graham, the Unite general secretary, said: “It is completely wrong that Reach is intent on making skilled print workers redundant, especially as its financial success was built on their backs.”

Aside from the impairment, Reach confirmed that the closure of two of its printing sites, in Watford, Hertfordshire, and Saltire, Scotland, would save £25m.

In its annual results, Reach reported an 8pc fall in page views and a 0.9pc drop in digital revenues. In the final three months of 2025, its digital revenues dropped by 7.8pc.

data.title: Top BBC executive quits as broadcaster races to cut costs
data.description:
The BBC’s chief operating officer has resigned, becoming the third senior executive departure at the broadcaster in four months, as the corporation pushes ahead with a cost-cutting drive.

Leigh Tavaziva, who spent five years at the BBC, told staff on Tuesday that she would be stepping down in September to “pursue new opportunities”.

In a resignation email, Ms Tavaziva said she would be leaving amid a restructuring of the BBC’s product and technology divisions, with the company set to combine teams from its public service and its BBC Studios commercial arm.

The reorganisation comes after Tim Davie, the BBC’s outgoing director general, announced plans for further cost savings of 10pc in the next three years.

A BBC spokesman said: “We are not announcing any cost saving measures as part of the announcement now.”

However, Ms Tavaziva said: “I do recognise the uncertainty many of you are facing with the changes we are announcing today.”

She added that combining the teams would help the BBC “focus our investment, make faster decisions, adopt shared tools and standards and innovate quickly”. It would also remove “duplication”, she said.

Ms Tavaziva joined the BBC from Centrica in 2021, taking on responsibility for operations and the technology behind products such as iPlayer. She received a salary of up to £470,000 in 2025.

The exit of its operations chief is the third senior departure from the BBC’s top team in a little more than four months.

Mr Davie and Deborah Turness, the broadcaster’s news chief, announced in November they would be stepping down after The Telegraph revealed instances of bias within the BBC.

An investigation by the newspaper found that a Panorama documentary had misled viewers with edits to a speech by Donald Trump, the US president.

Mr Davie is expected to depart in April, with the BBC hunting for a new director general who will be responsible for handling its negotiations on a new funding settlement with the Government.

The broadcaster’s current charter expires in 2027. Labour has said it is considering other ways to raise revenue, including part-funding by advertising or subscriptions.

Mr Davie previously pushed back on calls for subscriptions or adverts, arguing they would undermine the BBC’s public service mission.

The BBC has also been grappling with growing pressure from technology giants and an exodus of young viewers who have abandoned traditional television in favour of YouTube videos or TikTok.

In an exclusive interview with The Telegraph this week, Greg Peters, the co-chief executive of Netflix, said he would be “eager” to host more BBC programmes to help them reach audiences that had drifted away.

Netflix has been seeking wider deals with traditional TV channels, including signing up France’s TF1 and integrating it into its app menu.

The BBC signed a deal with YouTube in January to produce original shows for the app for the first time.

However, speaking this week, Mr Peters warned: “When you think about what a BBC production or a Netflix production costs, it’s not really supported by the YouTube model.”

data.title: The Traitors producer agrees £6bn merger with French rival
data.description:
The British production group behind The Traitors has struck an $8bn (£6bn) deal to merge with French rival Banijay Entertainment.

All3Media confirmed the tie-up with the studio behind Peaky Blinders on Tuesday, in a deal to create the world’s largest independent TV maker.

The combined group, called Banijay, will be responsible for producing more than 170 shows and events across Europe.

This will include shows such as MasterChef, Fleabag, Gogglebox and Call the Midwife.

RedBird IMI, which owns All3Media, and Banijay will be equal partners in the new joint venture.

However, the French business will receive €796mn in compensation owing to the fact that it is the larger company.

The deal will create a European media giant with distribution spanning nearly 250 countries.

This will put it in direct competition with Paramount, the American TV giant controlled by the billionaire Ellison family, as it prepares to take over Warner Bros in an $111bn deal.

Both companies had unsuccessfully explored a merger in 2023 when All3Media was put up for sale by its former joint owners, Warner Bros, Discovery and Liberty Global.

However, the deal was revived in the wake of All3Media being sold in 2024 to RedBird IMI, the Abu Dhabi-backed fund run by Zucker, which in November pulled out of its bid to buy The Telegraph.

All3Media is by far RedBird IMI’s largest asset.

Mr Zucker, who will become the group’s executive chairman, said the deal “brings together Europe, the Americas and Abu Dhabi as investors”.

“It is something that’s exciting to us because it gives us a really global outlook on the media ecosystem going forward,” he told the Financial Times.

The merged business will be led by Marco Bassetti, the Banijay chief executive, while All3Media boss Jane Turton will be named deputy.

The merger is expected to result in job cuts.

Speaking to the BBC on Wednesday, Mr Zucker said: “When there’s any combination like this there are duplicative roles and so we have identified the fact that there will be some cost synergies.

“Those are going to take place in things like real estate and procurement and duplicative roles in corporate function, but what we’re committed to is making sure that there’s no impact on the creative community or any of the production companies that come together as part of this.”

The deal is also likely to raise questions over the future of ITV’s production business.

Both Banijay and All3Media last year explored a £3bn takeover of ITV Studios but a deal did not materialise.

ITV is currently in talks to sell its broadcasting arm to Sky owner Comcast for £1.6bn.

If that deal goes ahead, it will likely need to seek another buyer for its production division.

data.title: AI is blowing up one of shadow banking’s biggest bets
data.description:
The stock market has become a hunting ground for future victims of AI, with seemingly daily sell-offs driven by fears that the technology will destroy business models.

At the forefront has been a slump in “software as a service” companies attributed to fears that chatbots will soon be able to replicate what they do. Companies ranging from Salesforce, the customer service software maker, to Palantir, the big data giant, have been caught up in the chaos.

But it is not just software companies – there are lenders hurting too.

When Blue Owl, the private credit behemoth, permanently blocked withdrawals from one of its tech-heavy private credit funds recently, it sent shock waves across the entire sector.

The move to gate the $1.6bn (£1.2bn) fund aimed at retail investors triggered a 10pc fall in its shares and dragged down other private credit providers along with it.

The announcement sparked fears of a broader crisis in private credit, a sector often referred to as shadow banking.

Meanwhile, the Bank of England is racing to understand the risks to the UK. It plans to conduct the world’s first stress test of the shadow banking sector to see how it would cope with a global shock.

But as it prepares for the dummy run, a real-life crisis is already taking hold.

Since the start of the month, private credit providers have been subject to a brutal sell-off. A closely watched index of 44 business development companies (BDCs), vehicles used by private credit players to extend loans, has shed around $5bn across February.

Funds managed by Blue Owl are among the worst affected, though other private credit providers including Sixth Street, Crescent Capital and Stellus Capital have also seen their share prices slide considerably. Barclays estimates that roughly a fifth of all loans held by BDCs have been made to the software industry.

“I think for private credit lenders and BDCs specifically, they tend to gravitate to industries that they believe are growing, that do not have significant capital expenditure needs and are less cyclical than other types of industries,” says Corry Short, an analyst at Barclays.

“Software did really check a lot of those boxes. It fit the underwriting appetite at a time when there was just a decent amount of deal flow.”

UBS has warned that private credit could see default rates surge to as high as 13pc in a worst-case scenario. As much as 35pc of the $1.7tn private credit market is exposed to disruption from AI, it added.

The rout is unlikely to abate either, the bank said, with parts of the credit market only in the “early-to-mid” innings of the sell-off.

Such forecasts matter beyond Manhattan, where Blue Owl and many others are based. Shadow banks are interwoven with regulated banks and pension funds that lend to them, meaning a crisis could quickly spread to the real economy.

It is not yet known how problems in private credit would precisely impact banks and pension funds, which is why the Bank of England is investigating. Because of the paucity of data, the crucial question of whether the sector poses a systemic risk to the UK’s financial stability also remains unresolved.

At the riskier end of the private credit loan market, issuances have fallen by 30pc year on year, according to UBS. Such a fall is often a harbinger of further downward slides in the wider loan market, the bank added.

‘We don’t have red flags’

As the UK has the second-largest private market after the US, there are concerns that ructions across the pond could have an impact in Britain.

Julien Conzano, an analyst at UBS, said an excess of capital and plenty of start-ups wanting to be the next Google led to the sector’s heavy backing of software businesses.

Craig Packer, co-president of Blue Owl, described the firm as “probably the largest lender” to private equity-backed software companies in 2023.

The firm has been less boastful since then. Marc Lipschultz, its co-chief executive, told investors in an earnings call last month that its software lending portfolio was in “pristine” condition.

“We don’t have red flags,” Lipschultz said. “We don’t have yellow flags. We actually have largely green flags.”

“The tech portfolio continues to be the most pristine amongst all of our portfolios.”

The reassurance did little to convince investors. Requests to take money out ultimately contributed to Blue Owl’s decision to limit withdrawals from its funds and wind them down. The private credit provider said it would make episodic payments to shareholders, and announced a plan to sell $1.4bn in assets across three of its funds to return cash to investors.

“There’s always a surprise in a credit cycle,” Jamie Dimon, the chief executive of JP Morgan Chase, warned last week. “The surprise has often been which industry [is impacted most].

“You didn’t expect utilities and phone companies in ’08, ’09, and this time around, it might be software, because of AI.”

‘Is software dead?’

Alastair Unwin, a fund manager at Polar Capital Technology, says: “There is a risk that the software itself is no longer the product; the intelligence it delivers is the product. Companies selling ‘tools for humans’ will lose to companies selling ‘agents that do the work’.

“In the most optimistic scenario, leading software companies successfully invest aggressively in AI capabilities to reinvent their products to address growing competitive threats.

“The best software and information services companies may navigate this period successfully and reinvent themselves, but history suggests the intervening period is usually uncomfortable.”

Shadow banks can’t say they weren’t warned. Concerns about the future viability of the software sector have been bubbling in the background for quite some time.

Last year, while rivals were leaning ever further into software, Apollo Global Management, the private equity giant, was rapidly cutting its exposure.

Addressing a gathering in Toronto last autumn, John Zito, the firm’s co-chief executive, was among the first to air concerns that AI might be coming for the software industry. He warned that it may be the biggest threat to private credit, rather than tariffs, inflation or higher interest rates.

“The real risk is,” he said, “is software dead?”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Recruitment giant nosedives as job market grinds to a halt
data.description:
More than a fifth has been wiped off the value of recruitment giant PageGroup as Britain grapples with surging unemployment.

Shares in PageGroup plunged by 20pc on Thursday as the company revealed a 60pc drop in profits following a hit from Britain’s ailing jobs market.

The company pointed to “tougher” trading conditions in the UK, recording an operating loss of £13.3m in the country.

PageGroup warned that companies in Britain were “deferring hiring decisions and candidates [were] cautious about accepting offers”.

The country’s jobs market is grinding to a halt, with employers bringing on fewer new staff in the face of soaring worker costs and stricter red tape.

Official figures show that the unemployment rate in Britain has risen to record levels over the past year, hitting 5.2pc at the end of last year. The UK’s joblessness levels recently overtook Italy for the first time since the depths of the financial crisis.

Bosses have blamed Labour policies for fuelling unemployment, with retail chiefs saying last month that higher worker costs meant they were planning to reduce headcount.

The Chancellor last year increased National Insurance for employers as well as pushing up the minimum wage. The wage rate is poised to go up again in April with Labour’s Employment Rights Act also coming into force, introducing new red tape for employers.

In her Spring Statement on Tuesday, Rachel Reeves admitted that unemployment would continue to rise this year, with forecasts showing it is expected to reach 5.3pc.

The Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR) said this week that it expected unemployment to peak at 1.93 million, a 12-year high, up from a previous projection of 1.8 million.

The spending watchdog warned that a variety of factors, including the growing use of artificial intelligence by companies, could push it even higher.

Stock markets have been rocked by the threat that more jobs could be replaced by AI tools.

Hundreds of billions of dollars were wiped off the software-heavy Nasdaq index earlier this year over fears that AI could replace work typically undertaken by traditional providers and professional services firms.

A report by Morgan Stanley in January found that Britain was already losing more jobs to AI than most other countries.

PageGroup said recruitment in the technology sector had been “challenging”, adding that hiring for non-permanent roles was proving more “resilient”.

It said it was collaborating with technology companies over using AI in its own business to “enhance efficiency, insights and customer service”.

However, PageGroup added: “Whilst technology and AI are powerful tools, we expect them to remain a vital supporting role to our consultants rather than a replacement.”

data.title: Morrisons to axe 100 more staff in fresh cost-cutting drive
data.description:
Morrisons has unveiled plans to cut up to 100 office staff as the struggling supermarket launches a fresh cost-cutting drive.

The grocer has announced the proposed redundancies as part of a plan to merge two divisions in its Bradford head office that source products for its convenience stores and supermarkets. 

By combining the teams into a single division, Morrisons said it would “remove duplication, simplify our store operations and capture efficiencies”.

As many as 100 jobs have been put at risk from the changes announced on Wednesday.

The threat of further job losses is the latest in a series of moves to slash headcount.

The supermarket scrapped a number of its cafes, in-store butchers and fish counters as well as smaller convenience stores across its estate in March last year, putting 365 workers at risk of redundancy.

It then sold its newspaper delivery division in November, in a move which resulted in 1,700 paperboys being made redundant.

The latest redundancy threat comes after Rami Baitiéh, Morrisons’s chief executive, said earlier this week that the supermarket was under pressure to cut costs after blaming the impact of Labour’s tax raid.

Speaking at the Retail Week X Grocer event, Mr Baitiéh said Morrisons needed to “unlock genuine opportunities that are worth £214m just to pay [for the extra taxes introduced by Rachel Reeves]”.

Mr Baitiéh claimed the tax changes from last year impacted Morrisons, which employs around 96,000 staff, “more than anyone else”.

This included Ms Reeves’s decision to increase National Insurance contributions for employers, alongside new recycling levies.

He added: “We have a lot of room to improve the cost without impacting the customer, without impacting the colleague and the shareholder.”

The Telegraph revealed last month that Morrisons’s latest cost-cutting efforts included putting dozens of pharmacies up for sale.

The supermarket launched a sale process for the sites after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies were not financially viable.

It followed another year of losses at Morrisons as it battled steep borrowing costs. Morrisons recorded £381m of losses last year, with its £281m debt interest bill having derailed attempts to turn a profit.

The supermarket has been struggling with elevated debt costs since its £10bn takeover by private equity firm Clayton, Dubilier & Rice (CD&R) in 2021.

It has been working to cut its debt bill by selling off some of its supermarkets before leasing them back. Property agents have now been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Following the proposed job losses, a spokesman for Morrisons said: “We understand this will be difficult news for them and will be offering them our full support, including helping them to find alternative roles elsewhere in the business wherever we can.

“There is no change to our convenience growth strategy and we continue to see the opportunity for significant expansion in the years ahead.”

data.title: Revealed: the best savings accounts on the market right now
data.description:
Regardless of why you’re saving – for a house, a holiday, your children’s future or as a buffer to cover emergencies – you’ll want to make sure your money is working as hard as possible.

To help you get the most out of your savings, Telegraph Money has launched a range of “best buy” tables showing the highest rates available across a variety of savings accounts. These tables update automatically every day, with data provided by Savings Data Limited.

It’s more important than ever to make sure your savings are receiving a competitive rate. At the end of last year, the Bank of England cut rates to 3.75pc, triggering many savings providers to reduce their own rates. And, given the latest inflation figure for January measured 3pc, it will become increasingly likely that the value of your money will be eroded by rising prices.

Our tables include the best cash Isa rates – whether you want an easy-access, notice, fixed-rate or variable rate account – and we also list the best Lifetime Isas if you’re saving for your first home or retirement.

If you have already maxed out your Isa, or want to save outside of the tax-free wrapper, we track the best easy-access accounts and interest-paying current accounts.

Those who prefer to save monthly should refer to the table of top-paying regular savings accounts, while savers happy to lock their money up for longer in pursuit of higher returns could benefit from our lists of the best fixed-rate bonds and notice accounts.

We also track the best Junior cash Isas and the best children’s savings accounts for parents putting money away for their family’s futures.

In this article, we will cover:

  • The best fixed-rate bonds
  • The best variable savings account rates
  • The best current account rates
  • The best Isa rates
  • The best children’s accounts
  • How to choose the right account for you

Why do rates matter?

Seeking out an account with a more competitive interest rate can make a huge difference.

For example, take a savings account paying a relatively low 0.75pc. A saver with £50,000 in this account will earn around £375 a year in interest. Moving the deposit to an account with a better rate can mean a difference of thousands. Take an account at the higher end offering 4.75pc – you’d yield £2,427 a year, an extra £2,052.

Cash loses value over time if it is left in an account that pays less than the inflation rate. At the current inflation rate of 3pc, a pot of £1,000 left in a zero-interest account would be worth just £970.87 after a year in real terms, and just £862.61 after five years. Our inflation calculator can show how your savings are affected.

What is a savings account?

Unlike a current account, which you’ll use to pay your bills and for everyday spending, a savings account is designed to be the place where you can stash your spare money away, where it will hopefully grow over time.

There are several different types of savings accounts – some offer variable rates, which can change at any time, while some require you to commit to locking your cash away for a fixed period.

In theory, the more inconvenient an account is, the more interest you should earn – but it’s not always the case.

The best fixed-rate bonds for 2026

Fixed-rate bonds tend to pay the highest rates on the market. Savers need to weigh up whether to take a fixed deal in order to get guaranteed interest for a longer term, or take a chance on a variable rate that could rise over the length of the term.

The best variable savings account rates for 2026

Variable-rate accounts tend to come with more flexibility than fixed-rate deals; often, you can make as many withdrawals as you like without taking a hit on interest, but some specific deals have their own restrictions.

In general, easy-access accounts let you take out money whenever you like; regular saver accounts require you to make deposits regularly; while notice accounts allow you to make withdrawals, but only if you give your provider a prescribed amount of notice.

By clicking through to each of our guides you can find out today’s best rates.

Easy-access savings account

Regular savings account

Notice savings account

The best current account rates for 2026

In some cases, current accounts will pay interest on the balance of money they hold, which can be useful if you don’t want the hassle of transferring money between multiple accounts.

The best Isa rates for 2026

Cash Isas work in a similar way to savings accounts, except that all interest you earn is tax-free – and you’re restricted to depositing up to £20,000 in each tax year. Income tax is payable on savings interest earned outside of an Isa, above an allowance of £1,000 for a basic-rate taxpayer and £500 in the higher-rate band.

Lifetime Isa

Savers who are under 40 and putting money away to buy a home or fund their retirement can open a Lifetime Isa. As well as the tax-free savings growth, the Government rewards savers with a bonus of 25pc, up to £1,000 a year.

The best children’s accounts

If you’re under the age of 18, or saving on behalf of a youngster, you can open a children’s account. Many account providers will let children add and withdraw money from the age of seven, so it can be a good way to teach financial skills early in life.

Children also get their own Isa allowance of £9,000 a year, which means an adult saving on behalf of a child in a Junior Isa can put this extra amount away on top of their own £20,000 allowance.

What is happening to savings rates?

Hundreds of savings accounts currently offer a rate that beats CPI inflation, which measured 3pc in January, according to the latest figures from the Office for National Statistics.

Savings have broadly been on a downward trajectory since they peaked in summer 2023 – but they’re predicted to fall more quickly this year, in anticipation of further Bank Rate cuts.

The most competitive rates can be quick to disappear – so if you spot an account that looks good, you may need to act fast.

Alice Haine, of online investment platform Bestinvest by Evelyn Partners, said: “For now, the best savings rates are continuing to outstrip inflation, giving savers who hunted out the top deals a healthy real return on their nest eggs. Locking in a top rate now before the best deals disappear could be a sensible strategy for those with cash languishing in an account delivering dismal returns to ensure their money is working as hard as possible.”

Use our calculator to see how much your savings account is earning you – and how much more interest you could get if you switched.

What is the difference between an Isa, bond, savings and a current account?

A current account is a transactional account that typically pays no interest but gives you a lot of flexibility in how often you access your money.

With a savings account, the bank pays you interest for keeping your money and therefore imposes some restrictions on how many withdrawals you can make.

A fixed-rate bond is a savings account with a fixed term, usually between one and five years. Until the duration of the bond is up, you cannot withdraw your funds, but in exchange for the commitment you will typically benefit from a higher rate.

The difference between an Isa and other types of savings account is that there is no tax charged on the interest. Everyone can save up to £20,000 a year tax-free in an Isa, or £9,000 for children.

How to choose the right account for you

The first thing to consider is whether you might need access to the funds in an emergency. A current account or an easy-access savings account will give you this flexibility.

However, you will get a higher rate if you are willing to lock away your funds for a set period (for example, in a bond). Generally, the longer the period, the higher the rate – however, this is not the case at the moment. Fixed-rate accounts with one and two-year terms are far higher than those with five-year rates.

The other thing to consider is whether you are at risk of exceeding your personal savings allowance. This is £1,000 for a basic-rate taxpayer and £500 for a higher-rate taxpayer. If you earn more than this in interest outside of an Isa, then you will have to pay income tax.

Use our savings tax interest calculator to work out whether you could breach your allowance, and if you should get an Isa.

data.title: Planning red tape makes mobile signal worse, networks claim
data.description:
Labour’s planning red tape is making mobile signals worse, Britain’s network operators have claimed.

Mobile UK, which represents BT’s EE, VodafoneThree and O2, warned that arduous planning processes were partly to blame for “frustrating” gaps in coverage.

The industry body said examples of red tape included so-called “notices to quit”, a legal demand requiring network operators to remove mobile masts when buildings are being redeveloped.

It said that while masts were quickly removed, the process of securing permission to construct a new site offering equivalent coverage could take 18 months or longer.

Mobile UK said around 7pc of mobile sites in London could be offline at any one time as a result of these hold-ups in councils.

Hamish MacLeod, the chief executive of Mobile UK, writes in The Telegraph that although ministers were attempting to tackle the problem, “longer-term, strategic planning” was needed.

He said Britain needed to “remove the barriers” to create more reliable connectivity.

Outdoor 5G coverage is currently available across 97pc of the UK from at least one provider. However, Britons have long been frustrated by sluggish speeds as networks struggle to cope with substantial capacity demands.

Research has shown that Britain ranks lowest for 5G download speeds in the G7, prompting complaints from MPs that patchy coverage is harming productivity.

Operators are deploying so-called “standalone” 5G – an upgraded version of current networks – to improve coverage.

They are also turning to satellite technology to help tackle signal “not-spots” in rural areas. O2 last week became the first UK mobile provider to launch its satellite service after striking a deal with Elon Musk’s Starlink.

Mobile UK argued that good coverage helped to stimulate economic growth and support public services, and that infrastructure needed to be in place before demand.

Mr MacLeod added: “What we need isn’t just for planning rules to catch up. Rather, we need them to leapfrog where we are now, so that they’re already in place for the next generation of technology.”

Britain’s mobile and broadband firms have long complained that overly complex planning rules and Nimbyism have slowed the rollout of new networks.

In 2024, the Conservative government wrote to broadband providers asking them to limit the installation of unsightly telegraph poles following a backlash from locals, particularly in picturesque rural areas such as the Cotswolds.

Efforts to deploy mobile masts in remote areas have also been hampered by complaints about the aesthetic and environmental impact of the infrastructure.

In one instance, the Scottish National Trust objected to the construction of a mobile mast close to the mountain resort of Glencoe months after a father and son were killed in a hiking accident in the same area.

The objections prompted a furious response from the industry, which questioned whether campaigners were putting Nimbyism before safety concerns.

At the time, mobile industry sources said that new network coverage could prove life-saving, although they said there was no guarantee that coverage would have saved the father and son.

Meanwhile, the rural 5G rollout is being threatened by a row with farmers, who have warned they will stop hosting mobile infrastructure on their land amid frustration over rent cuts.

The latest demands from the industry come after the Government opened a consultation on planning reforms in December.

Ministers have also launched a major review of the wider mobile market as part of an effort to ensure network infrastructure can keep up with surging demand for mobile data.

A government spokesman said: “Access to high-quality, reliable 5G networks is essential to boosting our economy and delivering services which are fit for modern life.

“Our call for evidence on targeted planning reforms is designed to accelerate the rollout of digital infrastructure.

“As part of this, we are considering measures that make it easier to quickly install replacement equipment and maintain network coverage – like allowing longer temporary redeployment after a notice to quit and expanding permitted development rights for telecoms operators.

“The findings will inform our next steps to reduce barriers in digital infrastructure deployment, including whether to consult and legislate in the future.”

Delivering infrastructure before demand will boost productivity

By Hamish MacLeod

The UK is already losing billions of pounds by delivering infrastructure too long after it’s actually needed. We must reverse the gap between demand and delivery.

In the 1926 film, The General, Buster Keaton precariously balances himself on the front of a train, removing railroad ties before they derail the locomotive on which he sits: the epitome of a “just in time” policy.

The General might have first been screened a century ago but the scene still serves as a useful metaphor about the need to deliver ahead of demand. The UK needs to drive growth but to do so we must ensure the right foundations are in place.

This applies across multiple policy areas but in infrastructure it’s particularly acute. Done well, digital infrastructure shouldn’t be built to “meet” demand but to stimulate it in the first place.

When it comes to major planning decisions, the UK’s record is noticeably poor. From the long-running saga of HS2 to massive data centre projects scuppered by a minor administrative failure, our system is a mass of hundreds of well-intentioned but accumulatively burdensome rules which slow down delivery and make it more costly.

For major projects, there’s no quick fix. The Government is showing signs of trying to grapple with the problem but it will take a determined effort to resolve. By contrast, amending rules to support smaller planning processes can be done very, very quickly. One such area is the deployment of mobile sites.

Digital networks are a critical piece of national infrastructure and one where the greatest productivity is delivered by ensuring it can be in place ahead of demand.

A slow data connection in a busy city centre is at best frustrating but at worst financially impactful for businesses through lost sales. Yet at present, around 7pc of mobile sites in London can be offline at any one time as landlords seek to redevelop buildings and networks struggle to secure replacement sites fast enough.

To be clear, this isn’t just about councils and planning decisions. It is about competing interests not always working in harmony.

A notorious example in the industry is “notices to quit”: legal demands served to commence the removal of an existing mast.

These are commonplace and part of a natural planning process that networks need to deal with, but timescales for finding and securing a new site that delivers equivalent coverage to the one lost, coupled with a full planning process to obtain approval, can take 18 months or longer.

There arises a conflict between a landlord or developer desperate to get a mobile site removed, often with the support of councillors, and a process of replacement not currently designed to be agile, resulting in a gap between the old site going offline and the new one working.

The Government knows this and is grasping the nettle. It has recently launched a slew of consultations to improve the environment for mobile infrastructure, as well as a wider mobile market review.

If they deliver the changes needed, it will help. But longer-term strategic planning is also needed.

For example, the introduction to proposed changes to planning rights talks again about connectivity needing to meet business demand, rather than the more ambitious goal of energising it.

Streamlining planning policy to support the more agile deployment of mobile capacity will allow that coverage to be delivered more quickly. Some of the main beneficiaries of this could be the very public services that sit in different council departments, allowing them access to secure, reliable networks over which to innovative.

It will also allow businesses to grow more quickly, no longer held up by poor connectivity. Research shows this is a multibillion-pound productivity opportunity.

As the UK’s three mobile networks seek to deliver advanced 5G connectivity, evidence suggests that could be worth up to £230bn.

Mobile networks – and digital infrastructure more broadly – should be there to stimulate demand, giving the capacity and speed that businesses need to innovate and compete with their global peers from day one.

What we need isn’t just for planning rules to catch up. Rather, we need them to leapfrog where we are now so that they’re already in place for the next generation of technology.

This lesson can be applied across many parts of the economy. From rail networks to housing, only by reducing the lag time between demand and supply can we realise the productivity benefits the UK needs.

As we enter an AI era, demand for reliable connectivity is growing rapidly and sizeably. We have to remove the barriers and support it, or the train may have already left the station.

Hamish MacLeod is the chief executive of Mobile UK

data.title: ITV insists Sky sale is still on as ad revenues fall
data.description:
ITV is racing to sell its television business to Sky for £1.6bn following a fresh drop in advertising revenues.

Dame Carolyn McCall, the channel’s chief executive, said the companies remained “actively engaged” in negotiations and insisted that the decline in ad revenues would not affect the price of any deal.

It came as the broadcaster behind hits like Love Island reported a 5pc drop in revenues for its television arm in 2025, though this was offset by growth in its production division. Overall revenues remained flat at £4.1bn.

ITV confirmed last year it was in talks with Sky’s US owner, Comcast, over a £1.6bn takeover of its television arm. But details of any agreement have yet to materialise and bosses warned there was no certainty a deal would be reached.

The broadcaster, which blamed Budget uncertainty for a sharp drop in ad revenues at the end of last year, said its performance was strong against a “challenging” market.

Dame Carolyn said it was too early to assess the impact of war in the Middle East on ad budgets and argued that there was strong interest from brands before the Fifa World Cup this summer.

However, an insider at rival Channel 4, which is also expected to suffer a decline in ad revenues, warned that broadcasters were facing growing uncertainty.

They said: “The ad market was volatile in the fourth quarter and has continued to be challenging in the first quarter, with macro issues like the situation in the Middle East prompting brands to focus on advertising on a month-to-month basis versus long-term investment.

“We’re seeing progress in our transformation with streaming minutes outgrowing the market – but there’s no denying it’s tough out there right now.”

Streaming rivals

The potential takeover by Sky is seen as key to building scale to compete with streaming rivals such as Netflix and Disney, which are increasingly drawing both viewers and revenues away from traditional broadcasters.

ITV said it had been boosted by strong growth for its streaming service, ITVX, with viewing up 16pc and digital advertising revenues 12pc higher.

But any transaction could also be complicated by ITV’s public service broadcasting obligations, including a requirement to provide news and regional programming to the ITV network.

ITV is also facing fresh speculation over the future of its more prosperous production division, which makes programmes such as I’m a Celebrity... Get Me Out of Here! and Disney’s Rivals. ITV Studios reported a 5pc rise in revenues to £2.1bn last year.

Two of ITV’s largest production rivals – French group Banijay and The Traitors producer All3Media – this week agreed to merge in an $8bn (£6.3bn) deal to create the world’s largest independent TV maker. Both companies had previously explored a potential takeover of ITV Studios.

Dame Carolyn played down concerns that the tie-up left ITV’s production arm without the scale to compete. She said: “We are very confident that we have scale globally. We’re not going to do something that you might say is transformative if it isn’t at the right value for ITV Studios.”

ITV, which has been reducing programme spend in an effort to shore up its finances, said it would make a further £20m of non-content cost savings this year. It has now made more than £250m of cost savings since 2019.

The broadcaster said it expected advertising revenue to decline by a further 2pc in the first quarter as brands hold back budgets before the World Cup.

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: GB News backers pump in more cash as news channel loses £22m
data.description:
The financial backers of GB News have pumped more cash into the TV station after it fell to a £22m loss.

All Perspectives, the broadcaster’s parent company whose backers include Sir Paul Marshall, the billionaire hedge fund manager, put £17.7m of new funds into GB News for the year ending May 2025.

It brings total backing for the channel, set up to challenge the BBC and Sky News and whose presenters include Nigel Farage and Sir Jacob Rees-Mogg, to £141m.

GB News said it remained “on track” to be Britain’s biggest news channel by 2028.

The broadcaster said annual revenues had grown by 58pc, from £16.6m to £26.2m, largely because of an increase in advertising revenue as its share of TV audiences grew.

Monthly viewers climbed from 3.1 million to 3.7 million while weekly radio listeners climbed from 517,800 to 547,300.

GB News narrowed its losses, which fell from £32.7m the previous year to £22m.

Accounts for All Perspectives said it had raised £29m in new debt from investors in the year to May, leading its debt to rise from £10m to £40m.

The debt has since been converted into equity notes which will become shares in the company in future. The parent company reported a £30m loss, which included financing costs.

A spokesman for GB News said: “The directors are confident about the future of the group and we remain firmly on track to achieve our ambition of becoming the UK’s biggest news channel by 2028.”

The company said revenue had been “driven by significant increases in audience share across TV and radio, alongside continued expansion of our digital platforms”.

“This audience momentum has translated directly into strong growth in advertising, digital, sponsorship and radio revenues.

“GB News and its parent company remain focused on sustained audience growth and a continued commitment to high-quality, independent journalism that is bold, fearless and serves communities right across the United Kingdom.”

A source close to the company said investors remained committed to backing GB News and it was moving closer to breaking even.

GB News surpassed the BBC News channel and Sky News for the first time in July and has recently pushed into the US.

It was fined £100,000 by Ofcom in 2024 for breaking impartiality rules with a programme featuring Rishi Sunak, who was then the prime minister.

However, last year it won a High Court victory against Ofcom, overturning a ruling that a programme featuring Sir Jacob had broken the broadcasting code.

Ofcom has also dropped a plan to stop sitting MPs from working as TV presenters, which GB News had called “irrational”.

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.
